title: Hartwell & Stone — Force majeure advisory: Hormuz transit and VLCC charter exposure

sections.executiveSummary: Hartwell & Stone proposes to act for BP plc on the legal response to the current Strait of Hormuz situation, focusing on the charterparty force-majeure framework across the active VLCC fleet. We have set out below a scope that delivers a defensible position within ten working days and avoids early dispute escalation with counterparties.
sections.whyNow: Tanker re-routings have begun and counterparties are signalling claims for late-delivery and demurrage. The window to invoke force majeure cleanly closes once counterparties start formal notices. Acting in the next two weeks preserves both the contractual position and the commercial relationships.
sections.whyUs: James Hartwell QC has led BP's previous Hormuz-related work and is recognised in the directories for energy and shipping disputes. Our disputes group sits alongside the international arbitration practice and energy transactional team in London, so the contractual review, dispute strategy, and any arbitration follow-through are co-ordinated under one roof.
sections.team: Lead: James Hartwell QC. Disputes support: senior associate (shipping) + two associates. International arbitration cover available on standby. London-led; New York for any US counterparty work.
sections.credentials: Energy / shipping disputes including prior BP charter work (2017–2018), Hormuz transit review (2019, this matter), and Suez Ever Given general-average recoveries (2020–2022). Recognised in Chambers UK as a leading firm for shipping disputes and energy litigation.
sections.scope: 1) Review the force-majeure clause in each active charter; 2) Draft and serve notices to counterparties on a co-ordinated timetable; 3) Provide a position paper on alternative-route obligations; 4) Hold an arbitration position in reserve. Excludes: pricing disputes, insurance recovery (covered under a separate engagement).
sections.fees: Fixed-fee phase 1 (clause review + notice drafting): GBP 850,000. Phase 2 (counterparty engagement and arbitration prep): time-charged at agreed partner / associate rates, with a soft cap of GBP 2.4m disclosed weekly. We will not exceed the cap without partner-to-GC sign-off.

title: Hartwell & Stone — Ever Given salvage and general average: recovery strategy

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to lead Maersk's recovery position on the Ever Given salvage and general-average exposures arising from the Suez blockage. Our scope covers cargo-interest claims, salvor negotiations, and the arbitration posture against the carriers, delivered as one co-ordinated workstream.
sections.whyNow: General-average declarations are crystallising and security calls will start within four weeks. A unified recovery strategy avoids inconsistent positions across the affected cargo interests and the carriers.
sections.whyUs: James Hartwell QC has acted for major container and tanker interests on previous SCAD and Suez claims. We have run the salvor-counterparty negotiation playbook before, including bond release timing, which is where most of the value moves.
sections.team: Lead: James Hartwell QC. Salvage / general average specialist counsel. Two associates on documentary review. New York liaison for the US-flagged cargo.
sections.credentials: Major shipping recoveries including prior BP / Maersk charter disputes and the 2018 Maersk Honam fire general average. Recognised in Chambers UK as Tier 1 for shipping disputes.
sections.scope: 1) GA bond and security strategy; 2) Cargo-interest co-ordination across the affected book; 3) Salvor negotiation lead; 4) Arbitration filing on standby if salvor terms unreasonable. Excludes: hull and machinery (covered by Maersk's hull H&M panel firm).
sections.fees: Time-charged with a monthly cap of GBP 320,000 across the matter team. Cap is reviewed quarterly with the General Counsel; partner sign-off required to exceed.

title: Hartwell & Stone — Acquisition of US biogas portfolio: cross-border M&A counsel

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to act for BP plc on the proposed acquisition of the US biogas portfolio identified, leading both transactional and regulatory clearance workstreams. Our scope covers the SPA, US merger control, and the cross-border structural elements that drive deal certainty.
sections.whyNow: The seller's process is on a tight timeline and rival bidders include at least two US strategics with first-mover advantage on the regulatory front. Filing readiness within six weeks is the gating constraint on the bid.
sections.whyUs: Catherine Stone leads our cross-border M&A practice and has acted for BP on three transactions in the past five years, including the 2024 Hormuz-driven asset reshuffle. We pair the London-led transactional team with US deal counsel through a long-standing referral arrangement that has cleared HSR filings inside agency review windows on every transaction.
sections.team: Lead: Catherine Stone. Senior associate on the SPA. Specialist counsel for HSR / FTC review. Tax and energy-transition counsel on standby.
sections.credentials: Recent BP transactions including the 2025 onshore US gas disposal advisory; HSBC European retail bank disposals (2023–2024); cross-border energy M&A for two other oil majors in 2022–2024. Recognised in Chambers UK as Tier 1 for energy M&A.
sections.scope: 1) SPA negotiation; 2) US merger control filing and engagement; 3) Tax structuring and IRA implications review; 4) Cross-border closing mechanics. Excludes: integration planning (covered under a separate engagement at close).
sections.fees: Step-fee structure: GBP 1.1m on signing, GBP 2.3m on regulatory clearance, GBP 2.0m on closing. Total cap GBP 5.4m. Side-letter caps for unanticipated regulatory second-request scenarios.

title: Hartwell & Stone — UK ringfencing structural review: implementation counsel

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to act for HSBC Holdings plc on the next phase of UK ringfencing structural changes, providing implementation counsel from the policy options through to PRA submission. Our scope addresses the structural, governance, and capital-impact workstreams in parallel.
sections.whyNow: The PRA's consultative window closes in late Q3 and HSBC's structural options need partner-level legal sign-off before management commits to a path. The cost of late-cycle changes (capital, governance, customer continuity) compounds quickly.
sections.whyUs: Marcus Webb leads our financial services regulatory practice and was on the original ringfencing implementation team for one of HSBC's UK peers. We sit alongside the leveraged-finance and disputes groups, so capital structuring and the litigation hedge are co-located.
sections.team: Lead: Marcus Webb. Senior associate for PRA engagement. Two associates on structural mapping. Counsel briefed for any contested points.
sections.credentials: Bank structural and regulatory work including SVB UK rescue financing (2022), JPMorgan First Republic resolution advisory (2023), Citi US consumer banking FRB consent-order remediation (2024). Recognised in Chambers UK as a leading firm for financial services regulation.
sections.scope: 1) Option-paper review and PRA submission drafting; 2) Capital-impact modelling input from the firm's CFO function; 3) Governance documentation refresh; 4) Customer continuity workstream legal cover. Excludes: tax structuring (covered under separate engagement).
sections.fees: Monthly fee of GBP 280,000 across the team for nine months. Caps re-set quarterly with the GC's office.

title: Hartwell & Stone — OFAC enforcement response: Iran-related correspondent banking

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to lead HSBC's defence to the OFAC enforcement action arising from Iran-related correspondent banking activity. Our scope spans the factual investigation, voluntary disclosure positioning, and the settlement negotiation, run as one workstream to preserve a coherent defence narrative.
sections.whyNow: OFAC's preliminary findings letter sets a 60-day response window. Settlement levers are strongest before the matter publicly crystallises; after that, the negotiation room narrows materially.
sections.whyUs: Robert Lin chairs our sanctions and trade practice and is one of a handful of UK-qualified partners with first-chair OFAC settlement experience in the past three years. We pair the New York and London teams seamlessly on these matters — the response is one team across two offices, not two co-counsel relationships.
sections.team: Lead: Robert Lin. New York senior associate on OFAC engagement. London associates on factual review. Forensic accounting partner for the quantification workstream.
sections.credentials: OFAC and UK OFSI advisory including Chevron Venezuela GL 41 renewal (2024), Citi cross-border OFAC compliance (2024), and the AUKUS supply chain advisory for Rolls-Royce (2022–2024). Recognised in Chambers Global as a leading firm for export controls and sanctions.
sections.scope: 1) Factual review and document collection; 2) Voluntary disclosure positioning; 3) OFAC engagement and settlement negotiation; 4) Remediation programme design. Excludes: any related UK OFSI cross-process (covered separately).
sections.fees: Time-charged with a soft cap of GBP 6.5m disclosed monthly. Cap reviewed at each phase gate. No success fees.

title: Hartwell & Stone — EU FDI screening on Chinese JV restructuring

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to lead Volkswagen AG on the EU FDI screening workstream for the proposed Chinese JV restructuring. Our scope covers the German BMWK filing, parallel member-state notifications, and the deal-conditionality work that feeds back into the SPA.
sections.whyNow: The transaction signing window assumes a clearance pathway is mapped before announcement. EU FDI regimes have hardened materially over the last 18 months; getting the filing strategy right pre-signing is now a deal-certainty question, not a paperwork one.
sections.whyUs: Henrik Vogel leads our German and EU FDI practice from Frankfurt and has run six BMWK filings on automotive and dual-use technology transactions in the past three years. We co-locate the FDI workstream with the SPA team so deal-condition language and filing strategy stay in sync.
sections.team: Lead: Henrik Vogel. Frankfurt senior associate on BMWK engagement. Brussels associate on EU peer-regime monitoring. London corporate associate as deal-team integrator.
sections.credentials: Recent automotive and dual-use FDI clearance work including Microsoft Inflection AI talent acquisition (2023), Ford EV battery JV South Korea (2024), and three undisclosed transactions cleared inside the BMWK Phase 1 window. Recognised in Legal 500 as a leading firm for German FDI.
sections.scope: 1) BMWK pre-notification engagement; 2) Filing drafting and submission; 3) Parallel member-state co-ordination; 4) Deal-condition feedback into the SPA. Excludes: US CFIUS (covered by US co-counsel).
sections.fees: Step-fee: EUR 480,000 on filing submission, EUR 1.6m on Phase 1 clearance, EUR 2.2m on full clearance (Phase 2 path if triggered). Caps reviewed at each phase.

title: Hartwell & Stone — EU export controls: semiconductor licensing review

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to act for ASML on the EU export controls licensing review for the latest generation of lithography systems. Our scope covers the Dutch licensing engagement, parallel EU regime alignment, and the customer-contracting workstream that depends on licence outcomes.
sections.whyNow: Dutch export-controls policy on advanced semiconductors continues to shift quarterly and the customer book in China and Korea cannot wait on regulatory ambiguity. Setting the licensing strategy now preserves both compliance and revenue.
sections.whyUs: Robert Lin leads our export-controls practice and has run ASML's US BIS licensing engagement (2023–2024). The London-New York-Brussels co-ordination is one team — and the team has worked together on the prior matter, so there is no relationship cost.
sections.team: Lead: Robert Lin. Brussels senior associate on EU regime co-ordination. New York counsel for BIS overlap. London associates on customer-contract workstream.
sections.credentials: Export-controls work including ASML US BIS DUV licence advisory (2023–2024), Rolls-Royce Saudi Tornado export licence renewal (2023), Northrop UK voluntary disclosure (2024), and the AUKUS supply chain programme (2022–2024). Tier 1 for export controls in Chambers Global.
sections.scope: 1) Dutch licensing engagement; 2) EU regime monitoring and alignment; 3) Customer-contract licence conditions; 4) Quarterly board-level update memo on the regulatory landscape. Excludes: any US-side BIS matter (covered under separate engagement).
sections.fees: Quarterly retainer of GBP 1.1m across the matter team for four quarters. Out-of-scope items priced separately and disclosed in advance.

title: Hartwell & Stone — CMA Phase 2 representation: Vodafone / Three UK merger

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to act for Vodafone Group plc on the CMA Phase 2 representation for the Three UK merger. Our scope covers economic submissions, hearings, and remedies negotiation, run as a single workstream with the in-house team's integration leads.
sections.whyNow: Phase 1 referral is the trigger; preparation for Phase 2 needs to start the day the referral lands. The economic evidence base on retail mobile competition is contested, and any delay narrows the remedies space.
sections.whyUs: Catherine Stone leads our UK competition practice and was first-chair on the 2023 Three UK / Vodafone preliminary engagement. We work with two specialist economic consultancies on a long-standing instruction basis, which removes the usual onboarding lag at the start of Phase 2.
sections.team: Lead: Catherine Stone. Senior associate on economic submissions. Two associates on document review and hearings prep. Specialist economic consultancy on standby.
sections.credentials: Recent merger-control representation including Microsoft Activision Blizzard CMA remedies monitoring (2024), Vodafone Italian unit disposal (2024), and Vodafone Spain disposal (2024). Recognised in Chambers UK as Tier 1 for UK merger control.
sections.scope: 1) Issues statement response; 2) Economic submissions co-ordination; 3) Hearings preparation; 4) Remedies negotiation. Excludes: integration planning (covered under separate engagement at clearance).
sections.fees: Step-fee: GBP 2.4m on issues statement, GBP 3.8m on remedies, GBP 2.9m on clearance. Total cap GBP 9.1m for the full Phase 2.

title: Hartwell & Stone — Securities class action defence: 737 MAX production guidance

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to defend Boeing in the securities class action arising from the 737 MAX production guidance restatement. Our scope spans the motion-to-dismiss phase, discovery, and any class certification work, with an option-paper on early settlement parameters in reserve.
sections.whyNow: The complaint window is open and lead-plaintiff applications close within 60 days. Setting the defence narrative early — particularly the materiality and scienter framing — shapes both the motion record and the settlement leverage.
sections.whyUs: James Hartwell QC leads our securities litigation practice and recently won the BP production-guidance class action (2024). The defence playbook for production-guidance restatements transfers; what changes is the FAA-overlap analysis, which we have separately briefed.
sections.team: Lead: James Hartwell QC. Senior associate on motion drafting. Two associates on document review. US co-counsel for the SDNY-side procedural work.
sections.credentials: Recent securities defence including BP plc production-guidance class action (2024, settled favourably), GSK SEC vaccine adverse-event inquiry (2024, closed without enforcement), and Bank of America MBS class action defence (2024, settled at 38% of demand). Recognised in Chambers Global for securities litigation.
sections.scope: 1) Motion to dismiss; 2) Discovery management; 3) Class-cert opposition; 4) Settlement option-paper at each phase gate. Excludes: FAA enforcement (covered separately).
sections.fees: Time-charged with quarterly caps disclosed to the GC's office. Total programme cap GBP 8.5m for the full motion-to-class-cert window; settlement-phase costs priced separately.

title: Hartwell & Stone — FTC AI investments inquiry response

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to lead Microsoft's response to the FTC AI investments inquiry. Our scope covers the documentary response, the leadership-level interviews, and the policy-engagement workstream that runs alongside, treated as one defence with the AI Act compliance team to maintain a coherent global narrative.
sections.whyNow: The FTC inquiry sets a 90-day documentary response window. Coordinating the response with the parallel EU AI Act and DOJ AI investments tracks within that window protects against position drift.
sections.whyUs: Elena Vasquez leads our AI regulation and tech-litigation practice. We have run the FTC tech-investigation playbook three times in the past 18 months. Critically, our AI policy team in Brussels keeps the AI Act position aligned, so the global narrative does not drift between jurisdictions.
sections.team: Lead: Elena Vasquez. Brussels AI policy senior associate. Document review team in London. US co-counsel briefed for any litigation overflow.
sections.credentials: Recent AI and tech regulatory matters including Microsoft EU AI Act pre-implementation impact assessment (2023–2024), Microsoft cloud competition EU DG COMP defence (2024), Google EU AI Act governance framework (2024), and Apple Intelligence AI regulatory framework (2024). Recognised in Chambers Global for technology, media and telecoms.
sections.scope: 1) Documentary production strategy; 2) Leadership-interview prep; 3) Parallel EU and DOJ alignment; 4) Policy-engagement workstream. Excludes: any structural remediation work, which would be priced separately if triggered.
sections.fees: Time-charged with a phase-1 cap of USD 3.5m for the documentary response. Subsequent phases priced at the next gate.

title: Hartwell & Stone — Trade-secrets litigation: ASML former-engineer dispute

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to act for ASML on the trade-secrets litigation against the former engineer named in the case caption. Our scope covers the preliminary injunction motion, discovery, and any criminal-referral overlay co-ordinated with Dutch and US prosecutors.
sections.whyNow: The information leak has crystallised in the form of a competing technical filing. Time-to-PI is the lever — every week the misappropriated material is in the public domain reduces the trade-secret protection available.
sections.whyUs: Elena Vasquez leads our IP litigation practice from London / New York and has run two prior ASML IP matters. The team holds the technical clearances needed to handle the underlying lithography material, which materially shortens the discovery curve.
sections.team: Lead: Elena Vasquez. Senior associate on PI motion drafting. Two associates on technical discovery. Dutch co-counsel for the criminal-referral overlay.
sections.credentials: Recent IP litigation including ASML US BIS DUV export licence advisory (2023–2024), AstraZeneca biosimilar patent enforcement (2024, PI granted in Delaware), Apple patent enforcement on wearables (2024, settled with cross-licence), and Merck Keytruda biosimilar enforcement (2024). Recognised in Chambers Global as a leading firm for life sciences and technology IP.
sections.scope: 1) PI motion preparation; 2) Discovery management; 3) Criminal-referral co-ordination; 4) Settlement option-paper at the PI hearing gate. Excludes: any related patent infringement workstream (covered under a separate engagement if needed).
sections.fees: Time-charged with quarterly caps. Programme cap GBP 2.4m through PI hearing; subsequent phases priced separately.

title: Hartwell & Stone — UK Energy Profits Levy: tax-structuring advisory

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to act for BP plc on the Energy Profits Levy advisory workstream, covering the structural response, capital-allowance optimisation, and the HMRC engagement plan. Our scope is sized to deliver a defensible structure within the current fiscal year and to leave the firm well-placed for any subsequent legislative changes.
sections.whyNow: The fiscal year-end is the gating constraint on capital-allowance positioning. Decisions taken in the next eight weeks lock the structure for the cycle; later changes carry meaningful tax cost.
sections.whyUs: Sarah Mitchell leads our energy tax practice and has advised BP on the windfall-tax workstream since the levy was first announced. We pair the tax team with the energy transactional practice in London, so structural decisions stay coherent with the wider M&A and decommissioning agenda.
sections.team: Lead: Sarah Mitchell. Senior associate on HMRC engagement. Two associates on capital-allowance analysis. Energy transactional partner on the structural side.
sections.credentials: Recent UK energy tax work including BP North Sea Forties decommissioning advisory (2022–2023), Shell North Sea decommissioning programme (2024), and GM US IRA structure review (2024). Recognised in Chambers UK as a leading firm for energy tax.
sections.scope: 1) Structural option-paper; 2) HMRC engagement plan; 3) Capital-allowance optimisation; 4) Quarterly board memo on legislative drift. Excludes: any non-UK windfall regime overlay.
sections.fees: Phased fee: GBP 450,000 on the option-paper, GBP 380,000 on HMRC engagement, GBP 300,000 on the year-end positioning. Total cap GBP 1.13m.

title: Hartwell & Stone — North Sea Forties pipeline: decommissioning advisory

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to lead BP plc on the decommissioning advisory for the North Sea Forties pipeline asset. Our scope covers the regulatory engagement with the North Sea Transition Authority, the supply-chain contracts framework, and the joint-venture realignment work that decommissioning triggers.
sections.whyNow: Decommissioning decisions trigger long-tail liabilities — the supply-chain framework and JV positions you set now still bind ten years out. The 2022 NSTA review cycle is the right window to align the legal structure with the operational plan.
sections.whyUs: Sarah Mitchell leads our energy and decommissioning practice. We worked on the BP North Sea windfall-tax advisory in parallel, so the tax and decommissioning structures stay aligned across the asset lifecycle — they cannot be advised cleanly in silos.
sections.team: Lead: Sarah Mitchell. Senior associate on NSTA engagement. Two associates on supply-chain contract review. Tax partner liaising on capital-allowance treatment.
sections.credentials: Recent North Sea and decommissioning work including BP Energy Profits Levy advisory (2023–2024), Shell North Sea decommissioning programme (2024), and Rolls-Royce SMR programme regulatory approvals (2024). Recognised in Chambers UK as a leading firm for energy and natural resources.
sections.scope: 1) NSTA engagement and approval pathway; 2) Supply-chain contract framework; 3) JV realignment; 4) Capital-allowance interface with the tax team. Excludes: any insurance / surety bond placement (handled in-house).
sections.fees: Fee structure: GBP 1.1m phase 1 (engagement plan), GBP 1.4m phase 2 (contracting framework), GBP 950,000 phase 3 (JV realignment). Total cap GBP 3.45m.

title: Hartwell & Stone — DMA gatekeeper compliance programme

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to act for Alphabet Inc. on the DMA gatekeeper compliance programme, covering the operational implementation, the conduct undertakings, and the parallel DG COMP engagement. Our scope is sized to deliver a defensible compliance package by the DMA enforcement window.
sections.whyNow: DMA enforcement actions have started; the compliance window is closing. Designing the package now — rather than reacting to a Commission decision — preserves both the operational shape and the negotiation leverage.
sections.whyUs: Dr. Ravi Chandrasekhar leads our EU competition practice from Brussels and has acted on three of the eight designated-gatekeeper compliance packages currently in the market. We sit alongside the data-protection group in London, so the parallel ICO and EDPB workstreams stay aligned.
sections.team: Lead: Dr. Ravi Chandrasekhar. Brussels senior associates on DG COMP engagement. London data-protection counsel. US co-counsel for any DOJ parallel-track items.
sections.credentials: Recent EU competition work including Microsoft cloud competition EU DG COMP investigation response (2024), Google UK CMA mobile-ecosystems probe (2024), Amazon EU DMA designation challenge (2024), and Microsoft EU AI Act pre-implementation impact assessment (2024). Recognised in Chambers Global as Tier 1 for EU competition.
sections.scope: 1) Compliance package design; 2) DG COMP pre-notification engagement; 3) Conduct undertakings drafting; 4) Quarterly operational review. Excludes: any follow-on damages litigation (covered under separate engagement).
sections.fees: Quarterly retainer of EUR 1.1m across the team for four quarters, reviewed against compliance milestones. Out-of-scope items priced separately in advance.

title: Hartwell & Stone — Zantac mass-tort defence: UK coordinated proceedings

sections.executiveSummary: We propose to lead GSK plc on the UK mass-tort defence in the Zantac product-liability claims. Our scope covers the strike-out application, expert-evidence strategy, and the parallel European coordination work, treated as one defence to maintain a coherent scientific narrative.
sections.whyNow: The lead-plaintiff applications are filed and the strike-out window is opening. Setting the scientific defence framing now — particularly the causation evidence base — defines the leverage for every subsequent procedural step.
sections.whyUs: Marie Dubois leads our class-actions and EU disputes practice from Brussels. She has run two prior pharma mass-tort defences successfully. The strike-out playbook for science-causation matters is well-established and transfers from those prior matters.
sections.team: Lead: Marie Dubois. Brussels senior associate on EU coordination. London associates on UK strike-out. Two specialist scientific experts on retainer.
sections.credentials: Recent product-liability and class-actions defence including AstraZeneca product-liability defence vaccine cohort (2024), Merck vaccine adjuvant MDL (2024, bellwether trial victory), Volkswagen diesel emissions German consumer claims (2024), and Sanofi Zantac follow-on consumer class actions EU (2024). Recognised in Chambers Global for product liability and class actions.
sections.scope: 1) Strike-out application; 2) Scientific expert-evidence strategy; 3) European coordination; 4) Settlement option-paper at each procedural gate. Excludes: any US-side Zantac coordination (covered by US co-counsel).
sections.fees: Time-charged with quarterly caps. Programme cap GBP 6.2m through strike-out; subsequent phases priced separately at each gate.
